cont.a.lead: Plugins are the official and only way to add new components: models, loaders, metrics, explainers, optimizers. Each plugin is an independent Python package published to PyPI, one-click installable from the UI.
cont.b.lead: Pull Requests to the repo are meant for fixes, refactors and new core features (UI, infrastructure, API, jobs system, file handling). You don't add new components via PR; that's what plugins are for.
cont.c.lead: Tutorials, academic citations, use cases, structured feedback, and community participation. It all counts and it all gets recognized.
cont.c1.p: Inherit from BaseModel, define a Pydantic schema, and the UI generates itself; you never touch React. Random Forest to transformers all fit here.
cont.c10.p: Changes to infrastructure, the frontend UI, the jobs system, HTTP APIs. Discuss it first in an issue before the PR: core changes require consensus.
cont.c2.p: Each loader is a subclass of BaseDataLoader declaring its format (CSV, JSON, images, audio). If your domain uses an exotic format, add it.
cont.c3.p: A plugin is a Python package that registers new components (models, metrics, optimizers). Users install it with one click from the UI, no restart.
cont.c4.p: If your domain needs a specific metric (PR-AUC, robust MAPE, a fairness metric), subclass BaseMetric and it shows up in the UI on its own.
cont.c5.p: BaseGlobalExplainer and BaseLocalExplainer separate XAI by scope. SHAP, LIME, counterfactuals, permutation importance, each as a dedicated abstraction.
cont.c6.p: Optuna and HyperOpt are already in; missing are evolutionary strategies, non-trivial QMC searches, and wrappers for distributed systems. Subclass BaseOptimizer.
cont.c7.p: The interface already supports ES, EN and PT. French, German and more languages via PR. You don't need to know Python, just the translation file.
cont.c8.h: Write a tutorial or cite the project
cont.c8.p: Tell us how you used dashAI in your course, paper, or consulting. Tutorials at docs.dash-ai.com, citable papers in BibTeX, share on Discord.
cont.c9.e: depends on bug | beginner to intermediate
cont.c9.p: Open issues on GitHub labeled bug. Great for a first contribution to the core: bounded scope, clear acceptance criteria.
cont.cit.lead: If dashAI was part of your research, citing it helps us sustain funding and keep growing the catalog. BibTeX and code of conduct below.
cont.coc.p: dashAI follows the Contributor Covenant v2.1. Respect, attribution, and honest technical discussion: that's all we expect. Confidential reports to dashai.nocode@gmail.com.
cont.lead: dashAI is open-source and is built in the open, with two clear mechanisms. To add a new component (model, loader, metric, explainer, optimizer), you do it as a plugin. To fix bugs, refactor, or add features to the core, you do it as a Pull Request. You can also contribute without code: tutorials, citations, translations.
cont.onb.h: Four steps to your first PR.
cont.onb.p: Shortest path: fork, add a new model, send a pull request. The UI shows up on its own.
cont.onb.s2.s: BaseModel for predictive, BaseMetric for metrics, etc.
cont.onb.s3.s: The UI is generated from the schema; you don't touch the frontend.
cont.onb.s4.s: The team reviews in under 48 business hours.
g.1.ctx: Each predictive model is a subclass of BaseModel that defines how to train and predict. The UI is generated from the Pydantic schema; you never touch React.
g.1.s1: Read the abstraction. Check BaseModel in DashAI/back/models/base_model.py to see the required methods: fit(), predict(), save(), load().
g.1.s2: Define your schema. Create a Pydantic class with schema_field() for each hyperparameter: type, range, default, description.
g.1.s3: Subclass BaseModel. Assign SCHEMA = MyForestSchema and implement fit(X, y) and predict(X).
g.1.s4: Register the model. Add your class to the __init__.py of the relevant subpackage (e.g. scikit_learn/, boosting/).
g.1.s5: Open a PR to develop. The team reviews in under 48 business hours.
g.2.ctx: Loaders convert files into typed structures. Each loader declares which formats it understands and how to validate them.
g.2.s1: Read BaseDataLoader. Located in DashAI/back/dataloaders/. Use the CSV or JSON loader as a reference pattern.
g.2.s2: Declare supported formats. File extensions, MIME types, default encoding.
g.2.s3: Implement load(path). Return a typed structure (DataFrame for tabular, dict of tensors for image).
g.2.s4: Implement validate(data). Report semantic errors in natural language, so the UI can surface them directly.
g.2.s5: Register the loader in __init__.py and open a PR.
g.3.ctx: A plugin is an independent Python package that registers new components in dashAI via entry points. Users install it with one click from the UI.
g.3.s1: Initial structure. setup.py with entry_points={'dashai.plugins': ['name = my_pkg:register']}. The register() function is the entry point.
g.3.s2: Import abstractions from dashai.base and declare your components (models, metrics, optimizers, whatever you want).
g.3.s3: Publish to PyPI. python -m build && twine upload dist/*. The package becomes available for hot-install.
g.3.s4: Share the name on Discord so others can try it.
g.4.ctx: Metrics are applied post-training. Each declares its scope (classification, regression, translation) and shows up automatically in reports.
g.4.s2: Assign SCOPE. Classification, Regression, Translation, etc. Determines where it appears in the UI.
g.4.s4: Optional: implement interpret(value) so the UI shows a natural-language description (e.g. "Good: > 0.85").
g.5.ctx: dashAI separates explainers by scope. Decide whether your technique explains the whole model (global) or a specific prediction (local) before choosing the base.
g.5.s1: Decide the scope. Global: feature importance, partial dependence. Local: SHAP, LIME, counterfactuals.
g.5.s3: Implement explain(model, X) returning the typed structure the frontend already knows how to render.
g.5.s4: Be model-agnostic when possible: wrappers like permutation/SHAP that work for sklearn, transformers and boosting.
g.6.ctx: Optuna and HyperOpt are integrated. Missing: evolutionary strategies, non-trivial QMC searches, and wrappers for distributed systems.
g.6.s1: Read BaseOptimizer in DashAI/back/optimizers/. Use the Optuna wrapper as reference.
g.6.s2: Implement optimize(model_class, search_space, X, y, n_trials). The search_space arrives pre-parsed from the Pydantic schema.
g.6.s3: Return a BestTrial with hyperparameters + score + history (for visualizing the convergence curve).
g.6.s4: Document your strategy in the docstring: Bayesian, evolutionary, QMC. Users will see it when choosing it in the UI.
g.7.ctx: You don't need to know Python. The easiest first contribution: copy a JSON file, translate, open a PR.
g.7.h: Translate the UI to a new language
g.7.s3: Translate the strings. Keep the keys (e.g. "models.train.button"), only change the value.
g.7.s4: Add your language to the switcher. Add the code and name to front/src/i18n/config.js.
g.7.s5: PR to develop. Attach a screenshot of the rendered language.
g.8.ctx: Three equivalent ways to contribute without code: document a use case, cite the project in a paper, or share results in the community.
g.8.h: Write a tutorial or cite the project
g.8.s1: Tutorial. Submit a Jupyter notebook or Markdown to docs/tutorials/ via PR. Suggested structure: context, dataset, dashAI steps, results, conclusion.
g.8.s2: Cite the project. Use the BibTeX at the bottom of this page. Helps us sustain academic funding.
g.8.s3: Share on Discord. Show what you trained, what data you used, what surprised you.
g.8.s4: Tell us an institutional use case by email: helps us document real-world adoption.
g.bug.ctx: Bug fixes are the ideal path for a first contribution to the core: bounded scope, clear criteria, and many new contributors have started here.
g.bug.s1: Find an issue labeled bug on GitHub. Prefer those tagged good first issue if it's your first time.
g.bug.s2: Comment on the issue that you're taking it, to avoid duplicated effort.
g.bug.s3: Reproduce the bug locally before touching code. No reliable repro, no reliable fix.
g.bug.s4: Minimum change that fixes the bug, plus a test that fails before and passes after. Don't mix refactor with fix in the same PR.
g.bug.s5: PR to develop referencing the issue (fixes #123). The team reviews in under 48 business hours.
g.feat.ctx: Changes that touch infrastructure, UI, the jobs system or public APIs. These changes require consensus before code.
g.feat.s1: Open a discussion issue first. Describe the change, motivation, and expected impact. Wait for team feedback.
g.feat.s2: If it's a new feature, align with the roadmap. Big changes outside the roadmap may be rejected even if well implemented.
g.feat.s3: Keep PRs small and focused. If your refactor touches 30 files, split into a series of incremental PRs.
g.feat.s4: Ship with tests + documentation. If you change a public API, update docs.dash-ai.com in the same PR.
g.feat.s5: Cross-review. Technical refactors go through two reviewers minimum.
g.lnk.repo7: See locale files in the repo
g.sec.lead: Each guide follows the same structure: one-sentence context, numbered steps, and a link to the corresponding file in the repository. The forthcoming docs.dash-ai.com pages will expand each one.
